title: Optical Response of a screw dislocated GaAs Quantum Wire: Temperature and Pressure Effects
authors: Vinod Kumar, Shweta Kumari, Surender Pratap
abstract: We investigate the influence of a screw dislocation, characterized by the dislocation parameter, on the optical response of a parabolic GaAs cylindrical quantum wire under the combined effects of temperature, hydrostatic pressure, and the axial magnetic field. Using a torsion-modified metric together with pressure- and temperature-dependent material properties, namely the effective mass and dielectric permittivity, we obtain exact solutions of the Schrödinger equation in terms of Whittaker functions. The screw dislocation introduces a \(k_z\)-dependent coupling that breaks the symmetry between the angular momentum states \(m\) and \(-m\) and modifies the centrifugal term in the effective potential. Based on the resulting eigenstates, we evaluate the linear and third-order nonlinear optical absorption coefficients, as well as the corresponding refractive index changes, for the dipole-allowed transitions \(m = 0 \to +1\) and \(m = 0 \to -1\). Our results show that increasing the dislocation parameter produces a pronounced redshift and suppresses the resonance amplitude for the \(m = 0 \to +1\) transition, whereas the \(m = 0 \to -1\) transition exhibits a blueshift accompanied by peak enhancement. We further find that increasing temperature shifts the resonances toward higher photon energies and enhances their amplitudes, while hydrostatic pressure causes a redshift and reduces the peak intensity for both transitions. In addition, the magnetic field strengthens the optical response and induces a blueshift for the \(m = 0 \to +1\) transition, whereas the opposite behavior is obtained for the \(m = 0 \to -1\) transition. We have also examined the behavior of the refractive index changes, which exhibit analogous asymmetric dependence on the dislocation parameter.
summary: This paper explores how structural defects like screw dislocations can be used to tune the optical properties of GaAs quantum wires. By manipulating temperature, pressure, and magnetic fields, one can control the symmetry and magnitude of nonlinear optical responses, which is crucial for developing advanced optoelectronic devices.
main_problem: The study investigates how a screw dislocation, combined with temperature, hydrostatic pressure, and an axial magnetic field, influences the linear and third-order nonlinear optical response of a GaAs quantum wire.
main_result: The screw dislocation breaks the symmetry between angular momentum states, causing a redshift and suppression for the m = 0 to +1 transition and a blueshift and enhancement for the m = 0 to -1 transition. Temperature and pressure further tune these optical responses through shifts in effective mass and dielectric permittivity.
method: The researchers use a torsion-modified geometric framework and density-matrix formalism to obtain exact analytical solutions of the Schrödinger equation in terms of Whittaker functions.
model_or_system: A parabolic GaAs cylindrical quantum wire with a screw dislocation characterized by a dislocation parameter eta. The model incorporates a torsion-modified metric and accounts for pressure- and temperature-dependent material properties.
key_observables: Linear and third-order nonlinear absorption coefficients, and relative changes in the linear and third-order refractive index.
important_parameters: Dislocation parameter (eta), temperature (T), hydrostatic pressure (P), axial magnetic field (B), and incident light intensity (I).
main_assumptions: The radial confinement is modeled via a parabolic potential; the wire is assumed to be much longer than its radius (L >> R); the axial wave number is fixed at a small value (kz = 0.01 A^-1).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the screw dislocation; Figures 7, 8, and 9 display the absorption coefficient and refractive index changes for different transition channels under varying external parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and dislocation model, presents the analytical solution of the Schrödinger equation, evaluates the optical response using density-matrix formalism, and analyzes the effects of temperature, pressure, magnetic field, and dislocation parameter on the results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic illustration of a screw dislocation in a GaAs cylindrical quantum wire, where the Burgers vector b = bˆz lies parallel to the wire axis and represents the lattice displacement caused by the defect.

Figure 2: Total energy ET for states m = 0, +1, and −1 as a function of the screw dislocation parameter η at ﬁxed cyclotron energy ℏωc = 2.5 meV. Solid lines: T = 10 K, P = 0; dashed lines: eﬀect of temperature (T = 300 K); dash-dotted lines: eﬀect of hydrostatic pressure (P = 20 kbar).

Figure 3: Transition energy ∆ET for the dipole-allowed transitions m = 0 →+1 (blue) and m = 0 →−1 (red) as a function of the screw-dislocation parameter η at diﬀerent temperatures and hydrostatic pressures. Solid lines correspond to T = 10 K and P = 0 kbar, dashed lines to T = 300 K, and dash-dotted lines to P = 20 kbar.

Figure 3 shows the transition energy ∆ET for the dipole- allowed transitions m = 0 →+1 (blue) and m = 0 →−1 (red) as a function of the screw-dislocation parameter η at diﬀerent temperatures and hydrostatic pressures. Solid lines correspond to T = 10 K and P = 0 kbar, dashed lines to T = 300 K, and dash-dotted lines to P = 20 kbar. The transition energy de- creases monotonically with increasing η for the m = 0 →+1 transition, whereas it increases for the m = 0 →−1 transi- tion, with the magnitude of the decrease being noticeably larger than that of the increase. Figure 2 shows the total energy ET of the angular-momentum states m = 0 (green), +1 (blue), and −1 (red) as a function of the screw-dislocation parameter η at ﬁxed cyclotron energy ℏωc = 2.5 meV. Solid lines correspond to the reference con- ditions T = 10 K and P = 0 kbar, dashed lines represent the eﬀect of raising the temperature to T = 300 K, and dash-dotted lines illustrate the inﬂuence of hydrostatic pressure P = 20 kbar.
